references.title: Contrasting prediction methods for early warning systems at undergraduate level

extracted.Preprocessing Details: Authors exclusively use the fine-grained data from the LMS of interactions with the folders on both Weekdays and Sunday.
extracted.Cluster Description:
Cluster 1 (n=6): Lower total views in general and, correspondingly, lower final result.

Cluster 2 (n=69): More views on Weekdays than on Sunday.

Cluster 3 (n=61):
More views on on Sunday than Weekdays, even though not by a substantial margin. 

Performance is indistinguishable for both of these clusters.
extracted.Implications: Cluster show that more activity tends to lead to better performance. However, whether the levels of activity come more during weekdays or weekends does not seem to have a significant impact.
extracted.Comments: Clustering via GMM is introduced as a new approach to feature generation rather than what is usually used.
extracted.Limitations:
ingle course Data

May not generalize well to outside STEM

extracted.Data sources: LMS Logs, Institutuional Data, Learner Results
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors collect information from the Student Registration System and  of the Student Interactions.

Each week has a specific folder that contains the materials meant for the week → thus there is, at max, a total of 15 stages.

Authhors also compare 4 feature configurations:

  1. Background, Continuous Assessment and Logs, 
  2. Background and Continuous Assessment,
  3. Background, Continuous Assessment and cumulative activity for each folder,
  4. Cluster variables
extracted.Models: Random Forest, BART, XGBoost, Principal Components Regression, SVM, MLP, Adaptive Regression Splines, kNN
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {MAE: 6.5 -  Week 6 BART with Cumulative Variables and Cluster Variables}
extracted.Comments:
Authors attempt do early prediction not only using the usual traininig paradigm on multiple features sets, but also try new approaches → cumulative variables and cluster variables on a Statistics course.

Results show that it is possible to obtain good predictions by week 6/15 using this approach,
extracted.Limitations:
Single course Data

May not generalize well to outside STEM

annotations.text: This work compares 8 distinct predictive algorithms to predict student performance (grade) in a statistics course. Authors also identify weeks 5-6 (pretty much halfway point) as the optimal time to make a prediction. Best model (BART) achieves 6.5% of MAE. Also, authors publish their code in a github repository.
